part_title: Section Six: Mechanical and Physical Control
part_subtitle: Mechanical and Physical Control of Tuta absoluta

items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

Mechanical and physical control comprise a group of interventions based on exclusion, isolation, organized removal of infested stages and their sources, or prevention of pest transfer among production units, without relying on the toxic action of insecticides.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mechanical and physical control comprise a group of interventions based on exclusion, isolation, organized removal of infested stages and their sources, or prevention of pest transfer among production units, without relying on the toxic action of insecticides.

items.text: This concept does not include every incidental manual practice or any procedure that cannot be measured and repeated. For a method to be included within mechanical or physical control, it should have:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This concept does not include every incidental manual practice or any procedure that cannot be measured and repeated. For a method to be included within mechanical or physical control, it should have:

items: A clearly defined mode of action.
Practical suitability for the production system.
Experimental evidence or reliable extension support.
No crop damage exceeding its benefit.

items.text: The methods with practical value against Tuta absoluta are concentrated in four principal areas:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The methods with practical value against Tuta absoluta are concentrated in four principal areas:

items: 1. Physical exclusion of moths using insect barriers.
2. Sanitary isolation of plants and suspected batches.
3. Selective removal of infested tissues and fruits.
4. Containment and safe disposal of plant residues and pest-transfer sources.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Physical exclusion of moths using insect barriers.
[[prepared]]2. Sanitary isolation of plants and suspected batches.
[[prepared]]3. Selective removal of infested tissues and fruits.
[[prepared]]4. Containment and safe disposal of plant residues and pest-transfer sources.

items.text: When used alone, these methods cannot provide complete pest management in most open fields. However, they play an important role in reducing initial populations, containing early infestation foci, protecting nurseries and greenhouses, and preventing residues and containers from becoming sources of the next generation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When used alone, these methods cannot provide complete pest management in most open fields. However, they play an important role in reducing initial populations, containing early infestation foci, protecting nurseries and greenhouses, and preventing residues and containers from becoming sources of the next generation.

items.text: Scientific Definition of Mechanical Control
Mechanical control is a direct intervention that removes living pest stages or plant parts carrying them from the production system through organized operations such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mechanical control is a direct intervention that removes living pest stages or plant parts carrying them from the production system through organized operations such as:

items: Removing plants from early infestation foci.
Shredding crop residues after crop termination.
Cleaning equipment and containers of plant material.
Transporting residues in containers that prevent spillage or pest escape.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Cleaning equipment and containers of plant material.
[[prepared]]Transporting residues in containers that prevent spillage or pest escape.

items.text: Its efficacy does not depend merely on separating the infested part from the plant, but on removing it from the pest life cycle and preventing eggs, larvae, or pupae within it from completing development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its efficacy does not depend merely on separating the infested part from the plant, but on removing it from the pest life cycle and preventing eggs, larvae, or [[term:pupation]]pupae[[/term]] within it from completing development.

items.text: Scientific Definition of Physical Control
Physical control is the use of a material barrier or isolation system that prevents moths from reaching plants or limits their movement among production areas.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Physical control is the use of a material barrier or isolation system that prevents moths from reaching plants or limits their movement among production areas.

items.text: Its most established applications include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its most established applications include:

items: Double-door systems and protected entry areas.
Isolation of new or suspected transplant batches.
Containment of residues and infested fruits during collection and transport.
Prevention of pest transfer with containers and equipment.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Isolation of new or suspected transplant batches.
[[prepared]]Containment of residues and infested fruits during collection and transport.
[[prepared]]Prevention of pest transfer with containers and equipment.

items.text: The principal effect of these methods is to reduce adult entry and oviposition opportunities, not to kill larvae already present inside mines.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The principal effect of these methods is to reduce adult entry and oviposition opportunities, not to kill larvae already present inside [[term:mines]]mines[[/term]].

items.text: Position of Mechanical and Physical Control Within IPM
These methods perform different functions according to crop stage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These methods perform different functions according to crop stage.

items.text: Before Planting

items: Protect the nursery from moth entry.
Prevent entry of contaminated containers or equipment.
Remove residues from the previous crop.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Prevent entry of contaminated containers or equipment.

items.text: During Early Crop Stages

items: Reject severely infested transplants or plants.
Remove plant parts containing confirmed infestation when economically justified.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Remove plant parts containing confirmed infestation when economically justified.

items.text: During Fruiting

items: Prevent accumulation of fallen and rotting fruits.
Separate rejected fruits from sound produce.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Prevent accumulation of fallen and rotting fruits.

items.text: After Harvest

items: Contain residues and move them to a safe treatment system.
Clean harvesting machinery, crates, and transport vehicles.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Contain residues and move them to a safe treatment system.
[[prepared]]Clean harvesting machinery, crates, and transport vehicles.

items.text: Physical Exclusion of Moths
Concept of Exclusion
Exclusion means preventing adult insects from entering a protected production area and is the clearest application of physical control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Exclusion means preventing adult insects from entering a protected production area and is the clearest application of physical control.

items.text: It primarily targets:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It primarily targets:

items: Fertilized females searching for plants on which to lay eggs.
Immigration waves resulting from removal of old fields.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Fertilized females searching for plants on which to lay eggs.
[[prepared]]Immigration waves resulting from removal of old fields.

items.text: Exclusion is most suitable in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Exclusion is most suitable in:

items.text: In large open fields, complete area isolation is usually economically impractical. Exclusion therefore focuses mainly on nurseries and on routes through which transplants and equipment enter.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In large open fields, complete area isolation is usually economically impractical. Exclusion therefore focuses mainly on nurseries and on routes through which transplants and equipment enter.

items.text: Insect-Proof Screens
Screens act as barriers separating the internal production environment from insect populations outside.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Screens act as barriers separating the internal production environment from insect populations outside.

items.text: Their efficacy depends on several characteristics, particularly:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Their efficacy depends on several characteristics, particularly:

items.text: Describing a screen only by the number of openings or threads per inch is insufficient because two screens with the same count may differ in actual opening size owing to differences in thread thickness and manufacturing method.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Describing a screen only by the number of openings or threads per inch is insufficient because two screens with the same count may differ in actual opening size owing to differences in thread thickness and manufacturing method.

items.text: Screen specifications should document:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Screen specifications should document:

items: Resistance to ultraviolet radiation and tearing.

items.text: Conditions Required for Successful Insect Screening
A screen provides effective exclusion only when the following conditions are met:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A screen provides effective exclusion only when the following conditions are met:

items: 2. No gaps occur between the screen and the structure.
4. Openings around hoses and cables are closed.
5. Holes and tears are repaired immediately.
8. Residual infestation from the previous crop is eliminated.
9. Residues are removed in closed containers.
10. Internal traps are used to detect any barrier breach.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]2. No gaps occur between the screen and the structure.
[[prepared]]4. Openings around hoses and cables are closed.
[[prepared]]5. Holes and tears are repaired immediately.
[[prepared]]8. Residual infestation from the previous crop is eliminated.
[[prepared]]9. Residues are removed in closed containers.
[[prepared]]10. Internal traps are used to detect any barrier breach.

items.text: The mere presence of a screen does not prove that the structure is isolated. The pest may enter with transplants, trays, or residues, or through a door left open.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The mere presence of a screen does not prove that the structure is isolated. The pest may enter with transplants, trays, or residues, or through a door left open.

items.text: Effect of Screens on the Internal Climate
Reducing screen-opening dimensions usually decreases air-exchange rate.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reducing screen-opening dimensions usually decreases air-exchange rate.

items.text: Depending on structure design and climate, this may lead to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Depending on structure design and climate, this may lead to:

items: Changes in sugar accumulation and fruit quality.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Changes in sugar accumulation and fruit quality.

items.text: Screens should therefore not be selected only for maximum exclusion, but through a balance between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Screens should therefore not be selected only for maximum exclusion, but through a balance between:

items.text: Preventing moth entry + maintaining a crop-suitable climate
The screen should be evaluated within the actual production system because the same screen may perform well in a well-ventilated structure and poorly in a low or poorly ventilated structure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The screen should be evaluated within the actual production system because the same screen may perform well in a well-ventilated structure and poorly in a low or poorly ventilated structure.

items.text: Partial and Complete Screening
Partial Screening
This includes fitting screens to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This includes fitting screens to:

items.text: It can provide adequate protection when the remaining structure is properly sealed.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It can provide adequate protection when the remaining structure is properly sealed.

items.text: Complete Screening
This means enclosing all openings and faces with screens while using a controlled entry system.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This means enclosing all openings and faces with screens while using a controlled entry system.

items.text: It may provide greater protection from external insects but may also have greater effects on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may provide greater protection from external insects but may also have greater effects on:

items.text: Increasing the screened area should not be assumed to produce a proportional reduction in infestation because results are also affected by transplant entry, door management, internal infestation, and barrier condition.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Increasing the screened area should not be assumed to produce a proportional reduction in infestation because results are also affected by transplant entry, door management, internal infestation, and barrier condition.

items.text: Double Doors and Protected Entry Areas
The entrance is a major weak point in protected structures.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The entrance is a major weak point in protected structures.

items.text: A double-entry system consists of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A double-entry system consists of:

items.text: The two doors should not be opened simultaneously.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The two doors should not be opened simultaneously.

items.text: The protected entry area performs functions including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The protected entry area performs functions including:

items: Reducing moth entry with air currents.
Holding a trap to detect incoming moths.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Holding a trap to detect incoming moths.

items.text: Doors should be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Doors should be:

items: Suitable for the size of carts and trays.
Not obstructed by crates or equipment.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Suitable for the size of carts and trays.

items.text: Management of Service Openings
Moths may enter through small gaps around:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Moths may enter through small gaps around:

items: Points where the structure meets the soil.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Points where the structure meets the soil.

items.text: These points should be included in the barrier-inspection program rather than limiting inspection to visible screens and doors.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These points should be included in the barrier-inspection program rather than limiting inspection to visible screens and doors.

items.text: Every defect or repair should be recorded with its location and date, and the nearest trap should then be checked to determine whether moth entry occurred.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Every defect or repair should be recorded with its location and date, and the nearest trap should then be checked to determine whether moth entry occurred.

items.text: Internal and External Trap Monitoring
Placing traps both inside and outside a structure helps assess exclusion efficacy.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Placing traps both inside and outside a structure helps assess exclusion efficacy.

items.text: Possible results indicate the following:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Possible results indicate the following:

items.text: High Catch Outside and Low Catch Inside
This indicates external pest pressure while the barrier remains relatively effective.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This indicates external pest pressure while the barrier remains relatively effective.

items.text: High Catch Inside the Structure
This may indicate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may indicate:

items: Presence of infested residues inside the structure.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Presence of infested residues inside the structure.

items.text: Internal catch should not be interpreted as screen failure alone before all entry pathways are reviewed.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Internal catch should not be interpreted as screen failure alone before all entry pathways are reviewed.

items.text: Isolation of Transplant Batches
Isolation is one of the most important physical interventions in the nursery.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Isolation is one of the most important physical interventions in the nursery.

items.text: New batches or batches received from external sources should be placed in a separate area before integration with existing production.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]New batches or batches received from external sources should be placed in a separate area before integration with existing production.

items.text: Isolation includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Isolation includes:

items: Inspection of leaves and growing points.

items.text: Isolation aims to prevent a single infested batch from distributing the pest throughout the nursery or among several fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Isolation aims to prevent a single infested batch from distributing the pest throughout the nursery or among several fields.

items.text: Rejection of Infested Transplants
A transplant should be rejected when it contains:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A transplant should be rejected when it contains:

items: Damage reducing its capacity for normal growth.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Damage reducing its capacity for normal growth.

items.text: Removing one infested leaf and automatically accepting the transplant is insufficient because other undetected eggs or larvae may be present.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Removing one infested leaf and automatically accepting the transplant is insufficient because other undetected eggs or larvae may be present.

items.text: When infestation occurs in more than one tray or several larval instars are found, the entire batch should be assessed, not only visibly infested plants.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When infestation occurs in more than one tray or several [[term:larval-instar]]larval instars[[/term]] are found, the entire batch should be assessed, not only visibly infested plants.

items.text: Removal of Infested Focus Plants in the Field
A whole plant may be removed when it represents a limited early-season focus and contains:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A whole plant may be removed when it represents a limited early-season focus and contains:

items.text: Whole-plant removal is most useful when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Whole-plant removal is most useful when:

items: The number of infested plants is limited.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The number of infested plants is limited.

items.text: This method is unsuitable once infestation has spread through a large proportion of the field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This method is unsuitable once infestation has spread through a large proportion of the field.

items.text: Selective Removal of Infested Plant Parts
Selective removal is used to remove eggs or larvae from the production system before they complete development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Selective removal is used to remove eggs or larvae from the production system before they complete development.

items.text: It may include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may include:

items: A bud or tender stem section.

items.text: The operation is scientifically valid when it is based on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The operation is scientifically valid when it is based on:

items: Estimation of the number of parts removed.
Collection of removed parts in a container.
Comparison of removal cost with the value of crop protected.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Estimation of the number of parts removed.
[[prepared]]Collection of removed parts in a container.
[[prepared]]Comparison of removal cost with the value of crop protected.

items.text: Random removal of every leaf carrying an old mine is neither scientific nor economical.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Random removal of every leaf carrying an old mine is neither scientific nor economical.

items.text: Limits of Leaf Removal
Selective removal should not become extensive defoliation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Selective removal should not become extensive defoliation.

items.text: Suitability depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Suitability depends on:

items: Proximity of leaves to fruit clusters.

items.text: Risks of leaf removal are greater in determinate processing tomatoes because the canopy contributes to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Risks of leaf removal are greater in determinate processing tomatoes because the canopy contributes to:

items: Protection of fruits from direct radiation.

items.text: Removal should therefore be limited to severely infested parts within early infestation foci.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Removal should therefore be limited to severely infested parts within early infestation foci.

items.text: Handling Parasitized Eggs
An infested leaf may carry eggs parasitized by egg parasitoids.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An infested leaf may carry eggs [[term:biological-control]]parasitized[[/term]] by egg parasitoids.

items.text: Before removing the leaf, the following should be distinguished:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Before removing the leaf, the following should be distinguished:

items.text: Disposal of leaves carrying parasitized eggs may eliminate beneficial parasitoid individuals.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Disposal of leaves carrying parasitized eggs may eliminate beneficial parasitoid individuals.

items.text: When an egg-parasitoid release program is in place, mechanical removal should be coordinated with the release program so that a large proportion of parasitoids is not removed before emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When an egg-parasitoid release program is in place, mechanical removal should be coordinated with the release program so that a large proportion of parasitoids is not removed before emergence.

items.text: Collection of Infested Fruits
Collection of infested fruits is one of the most important mechanical interventions after fruiting begins.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Collection of infested fruits is one of the most important mechanical interventions after fruiting begins.

items.text: Fruits should be collected when they bear:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fruits should be collected when they bear:

items.text: Collection achieves two objectives:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Collection achieves two objectives:

items: 1. Reducing the proportion of rotted and rejected fruits within the field.
2. Preventing larvae inside fruits from completing development or exiting to pupate.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Reducing the proportion of rotted and rejected fruits within the field.
[[prepared]]2. Preventing larvae inside fruits from completing development or exiting to pupate.

items.text: Rejected fruits should not be left between rows or at loading points.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Rejected fruits should not be left between rows or at loading points.

items.text: Collection of Infested Processing-Tomato Fruits
In processing tomatoes, selective collection should be implemented when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In processing tomatoes, selective collection should be implemented when:

items: Labor cost is lower than the expected loss.
Infested fruits represent a source of rots or larvae.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Labor cost is lower than the expected loss.
[[prepared]]Infested fruits represent a source of rots or larvae.

items.text: The following should be recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded:

items.text: Weight loss is calculated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Weight loss is calculated as:

items.text: Weight loss (%) = Weight of infested and rejected fruits ÷ Total sample weight × 100
This indicator is used to evaluate whether manual collection remains economically justified or whether another intervention is required.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This indicator is used to evaluate whether manual collection remains economically justified or whether another intervention is required.

items.text: Containers for Collecting Infested Material
Containers used to collect infested leaves and fruits should be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Containers used to collect infested leaves and fruits should be:

items: Clearly marked by color or label.
Designed to prevent loss of contents.

items.text: Open piles should not be used as temporary collection points because larvae may leave the tissues, while pupae or moths may continue development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Open piles should not be used as temporary collection points because larvae may leave the tissues, while pupae or moths may continue development.

items.text: Crop-Residue Management
Tomato residues after harvest are a source of eggs, larvae, and pupae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tomato residues after harvest are a source of eggs, larvae, and pupae.

items.text: Residues include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Residues include:

items: Sorting waste discarded in the field.

items.text: The crop should be terminated within a short period after economic harvesting stops because continued green growth allows the pest to reproduce and produce moths that move into the next planting season.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The crop should be terminated within a short period after economic harvesting stops because continued green growth allows the pest to reproduce and produce moths that move into the next planting season.

items.text: Postharvest Residue Removal
Scientific residue removal consists of an integrated sequence:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Scientific residue removal consists of an integrated sequence:

items: 5. Move it to a safe treatment system or manage it on-site in a manner that prevents pest survival.
7. Continue monitoring the field with traps.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]5. Move it to a safe treatment system or manage it on-site in a manner that prevents pest survival.
[[prepared]]7. Continue monitoring the field with traps.

items.text: Implementing only one step while leaving the rest of the residues unmanaged is insufficient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Implementing only one step while leaving the rest of the residues unmanaged is insufficient.

items.text: Shredding as a Preparatory Stage
Shredding:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Shredding:

items: Stops the intact plant from continuing as a host.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Stops the intact plant from continuing as a host.

items.text: However, shredding should not be regarded as a stand-alone guarantee that all stages are killed because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, shredding should not be regarded as a stand-alone guarantee that all stages are killed because:

items: Larvae may remain inside fruits that were not collected.
Pupae may occur in the soil.
Large plant pieces may remain uncut.
Individuals may have already left the plant material.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Larvae may remain inside fruits that were not collected.
[[prepared]]Individuals may have already left the plant material.

items.text: Shredding should therefore be used as a preparatory stage preceding containment, treatment, or soil cultivation, rather than as the final step.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Shredding should therefore be used as a preparatory stage preceding containment, treatment, or soil cultivation, rather than as the final step.

items.text: Evidence Supporting Residue Removal
A recent field study showed that fields in which tomato residues and weeds were left after harvest retained higher moth densities than fields that were cleaned and subjected to soil cultivation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A recent field study showed that fields in which tomato residues and weeds were left after harvest retained higher moth densities than fields that were cleaned and subjected to soil cultivation.

items.text: This result supports the need to terminate old fields and prevent residues and green hosts from persisting between seasons.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This result supports the need to terminate old fields and prevent residues and green hosts from persisting between seasons.

items.text: However, the study should be interpreted as an evaluation of a package of measures including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, the study should be interpreted as an evaluation of a package of measures including:

items.text: The independent effect of each measure cannot therefore be separated from the available results.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The independent effect of each measure cannot therefore be separated from the available results.

items.text: Sanitary Disposal of Residues
Sanitary disposal means using a pathway that prevents living pest stages in the residues from returning to the field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Sanitary disposal means using a pathway that prevents living pest stages in the residues from returning to the field.

items.text: Acceptable pathways, depending on available resources and regulations, include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Acceptable pathways, depending on available resources and regulations, include:

items: Transport to a closed treatment facility.
Containment within a closed system until tissues decompose.
Burial or treatment according to local guidance and requirements.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Containment within a closed system until tissues decompose.
[[prepared]]Burial or treatment according to local guidance and requirements.

items.text: Discarding residues in an open pile, watercourse, or neighboring area is not sanitary disposal.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Discarding residues in an open pile, watercourse, or neighboring area is not sanitary disposal.

items.text: Controlled Thermophilic Composting
Tomato residues may be included in an organic-composting system when the system can:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tomato residues may be included in an organic-composting system when the system can:

items: Generate uniform heat throughout the pile.
Prevent insect escape before treatment is completed.
Prevent subsequent germination of tomato seeds.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Prevent insect escape before treatment is completed.

items.text: Not every compost pile can be assumed to kill Tuta stages. A cold or uneven pile may allow larvae or pupae to survive or moths to emerge.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Not every compost pile can be assumed to kill Tuta stages. A cold or uneven pile may allow larvae or pupae to survive or moths to emerge.

items.text: The process should be treated as a treatment system, not merely a residue-collection site.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The process should be treated as a treatment system, not merely a residue-collection site.

items.text: Residues from Sorting Stations and Processing Plants
Sorting stations and tomato-processing plants become risk points when the following accumulate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Sorting stations and tomato-processing plants become risk points when the following accumulate:

items: Seeds capable of producing volunteer plants.

items.text: The management plan should include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The management plan should include:

items: Collection of residues in a designated area.
Prevention of exposed accumulation near fields.
Monitoring the site with traps when cultivated fields are nearby.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Collection of residues in a designated area.
[[prepared]]Monitoring the site with traps when cultivated fields are nearby.

items.text: Cleaning Crates and Containers
Containers can transfer the pest when they contain:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Containers can transfer the pest when they contain:

items.text: Cleaning consists of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cleaning consists of:

items: 6. Storing containers away from residue piles.
7. Preventing their use for transplant transport before cleaning.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]6. Storing containers away from residue piles.
[[prepared]]7. Preventing their use for transplant transport before cleaning.

items.text: Containers should not be moved directly from a heavily infested old field into a nursery or new crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Containers should not be moved directly from a heavily infested old field into a nursery or new crop.

items.text: Cleaning Machinery and Transport Vehicles
Cleaning should include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cleaning should include:

items.text: Particular attention should be given to sites where the following accumulate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Particular attention should be given to sites where the following accumulate:

items.text: Cleaning waste should be collected and treated as infested residue rather than discarded in the next field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cleaning waste should be collected and treated as infested residue rather than discarded in the next field.

items.text: Containment of Residues During Transport
Residues should be transported in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Residues should be transported in:

items.text: Containment aims to prevent:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Containment aims to prevent:

items: Establishment of volunteer tomato plants along roads and unloading areas.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Establishment of volunteer tomato plants along roads and unloading areas.

items.text: Mechanical Control in the Nursery
Mechanical methods are more applicable in nurseries because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mechanical methods are more applicable in nurseries because of:

items.text: Basic measures include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Basic measures include:

items: Preventing surplus transplants from remaining after the gap-filling period.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Preventing surplus transplants from remaining after the gap-filling period.

items.text: The nursery objective is to produce pest-free transplants, not merely to reduce pest abundance.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The nursery objective is to produce pest-free transplants, not merely to reduce pest abundance.

items.text: Mechanical Control in Greenhouses
This includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This includes:

items: Removal of infested leaves and fruits in closed containers.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Removal of infested leaves and fruits in closed containers.

items.text: These methods are generally more effective in greenhouses than in open fields because the structure has defined boundaries that can be isolated and monitored.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These methods are generally more effective in greenhouses than in open fields because the structure has defined boundaries that can be isolated and monitored.

items.text: Mechanical Control in Open Fields
Economically useful measures in open fields focus on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Economically useful measures in open fields focus on:

items.text: Large-scale removal of infested leaves or attempts to create barriers over extensive processing-tomato fields are generally of limited economic and practical value.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Large-scale removal of infested leaves or attempts to create barriers over extensive processing-tomato fields are generally of limited economic and practical value.

items.text: Compatibility with Biological Control
Barriers and organized removal have little direct toxic effect on natural enemies, but they may reduce their numbers indirectly when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Barriers and organized removal have little direct toxic effect on natural enemies, but they may reduce their numbers indirectly when:

items: Leaves carrying parasitized eggs are removed.
Plant parts bearing predators are discarded.
Screens prevent natural enemies from entering the structure.
Temperature and humidity change inside the greenhouse.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Screens prevent natural enemies from entering the structure.
[[prepared]]Temperature and humidity change inside the greenhouse.

items.text: They should therefore be integrated with biological-control programs by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They should therefore be integrated with biological-control programs by:

items: Retaining plant parts containing successful parasitoids when crop condition permits.
Releasing natural enemies inside isolated structures.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Retaining plant parts containing successful parasitoids when crop condition permits.

items.text: Evaluation of Efficacy
Efficacy of mechanical and physical control should not be measured by the number of plant parts removed, but by its effect on pest populations and the crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Efficacy of mechanical and physical control should not be measured by the number of plant parts removed, but by its effect on pest populations and the crop.

items.text: Evaluation indicators include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Evaluation indicators include:

items: Reduction in the percentage of infested fruits.
Reduction in trap catches inside protected structures.
Reduced transfer of the pest into the next crop.
Absence of serious deterioration in ventilation or fruit quality.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reduction in the percentage of infested fruits.
[[prepared]]Reduction in trap catches inside protected structures.
[[prepared]]Reduced transfer of the pest into the next crop.
[[prepared]]Absence of serious deterioration in ventilation or fruit quality.

items.text: Evaluation of Screens and Barriers
Barriers should be evaluated using a combination of indicators including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Barriers should be evaluated using a combination of indicators including:

items: Moth numbers outside and inside the structure.
Eggs and larvae within the crop.
Occurrence of diseases associated with high humidity.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Moth numbers outside and inside the structure.
[[prepared]]Occurrence of diseases associated with high humidity.

items.text: A barrier should not be considered successful when it reduces Tuta entry but causes substantial losses in fruit set or quality because of poor ventilation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A barrier should not be considered successful when it reduces Tuta entry but causes substantial losses in fruit set or quality because of poor ventilation.

items.text: Indicators of Successful Focus Removal
Focus removal is considered successful when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Focus removal is considered successful when:

items: No new mines appear on neighboring plants.
The infested area does not expand.
Growing-point or fruit infestation does not appear.
The focus does not require repeated uneconomic removal.
Major canopy loss does not occur.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]No new mines appear on neighboring plants.
[[prepared]]Growing-point or fruit infestation does not appear.
[[prepared]]The focus does not require repeated uneconomic removal.

items.text: If infestation continues after removal, this may indicate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]If infestation continues after removal, this may indicate:

items: Infestation extending beyond the identified focus.

items.text: Levels of Use According to Production System
Common Errors
Error One: Classifying Any Manual Practice as Mechanical Control
The method should be measurable and have a clear objective.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The method should be measurable and have a clear objective.

items.text: Error Two: Removing an Infested Leaf and Leaving It in the Field
The pest cycle is not interrupted if the larva can continue developing.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pest cycle is not interrupted if the larva can continue developing.

items.text: Error Three: Removing All Leaves Carrying Mines
Old mines do not necessarily represent active infestation, and excessive removal damages the crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Old mines do not necessarily represent active infestation, and excessive removal damages the crop.

items.text: Error Four: Relying on Screens Without Inspecting Transplants
The pest may enter the structure with plants.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pest may enter the structure with plants.

items.text: Error Five: Relying on Thread Count Instead of Actual Opening Size
Screen specifications and air permeability should be evaluated.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Screen specifications and air permeability should be evaluated.

items.text: Error Six: Tightening Screening While Neglecting Ventilation
Physiological or disease-related losses may occur.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Physiological or disease-related losses may occur.

items.text: Error Seven: Leaving Doors Open
This weakens the physical barrier regardless of screen quality.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This weakens the physical barrier regardless of screen quality.

items.text: Error Eight: Treating Shredding as the Final Residue Treatment
Shredding is a preparatory stage and should be followed by appropriate containment or treatment.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Shredding is a preparatory stage and should be followed by appropriate containment or treatment.

items.text: Error Nine: Transporting Residues in Open Vehicles
This causes fruits and leaves to fall and spreads the pest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This causes fruits and leaves to fall and spreads the pest.

items.text: Error Ten: Moving Harvest Crates into the Nursery Without Cleaning
Crates may carry plant material and living pest stages.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Crates may carry plant material and living pest stages.

items.text: Error Eleven: Disposing of Residues in an Open Pile
The pile may become a source of pupae, moths, and volunteer plants.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pile may become a source of pupae, moths, and volunteer plants.

items.text: Error Twelve: Applying Manual Removal After Infestation Has Spread
Economic feasibility declines rapidly as field area and pest density increase.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Economic feasibility declines rapidly as field area and pest density increase.

items.text: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
The practical priorities are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The practical priorities are:

items: 1. Inspect transplants and isolate batches before transplanting.
3. Contain the first infestation foci before they spread.
4. Avoid removing a large leaf area from processing tomatoes.
5. Collect infested fruits and do not leave them between rows.
6. Allocate dedicated containers for infested material.
7. Terminate the field as soon as harvest is no longer economically viable.
8. Collect remaining fruits before shredding plants.
9. Use shredding as one stage within a disposal system, not as a stand-alone measure.
11. Clean harvesting machinery and crates before moving them into another field.
12. Manage sorting-station and processing-plant residues within a closed system.
13. Use screens and controlled doors in nurseries.
14. Compare trap catches inside and outside the nursery.
15. Evaluate the effect of screens on ventilation and transplant quality before adoption.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Inspect transplants and isolate batches before transplanting.
[[prepared]]3. Contain the first infestation foci before they spread.
[[prepared]]4. Avoid removing a large leaf area from processing tomatoes.
[[prepared]]5. Collect infested fruits and do not leave them between rows.
[[prepared]]6. Allocate dedicated containers for infested material.
[[prepared]]7. Terminate the field as soon as harvest is no longer economically viable.
[[prepared]]8. Collect remaining fruits before shredding plants.
[[prepared]]9. Use shredding as one stage within a disposal system, not as a stand-alone measure.
[[prepared]]11. Clean harvesting machinery and crates before moving them into another field.
[[prepared]]12. Manage sorting-station and processing-plant residues within a closed system.
[[prepared]]13. Use screens and controlled doors in nurseries.
[[prepared]]14. Compare trap catches inside and outside the nursery.
[[prepared]]15. Evaluate the effect of screens on ventilation and transplant quality before adoption.

items.text: Table of Scientifically Supported Methods

rows: Prevent completion of development and pupation

items.text: Summary of Section Six
Scientifically based mechanical and physical control of Tuta absoluta depends on exclusion, isolation, organized removal, and sanitary containment of crop residues.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Scientifically based mechanical and physical control of Tuta absoluta depends on exclusion, isolation, organized removal, and sanitary containment of crop residues.

items.text: Insect-proof screens and properly sealed entrances are among the most important exclusion tools in nurseries and greenhouses, but their efficacy depends on installation quality, door management, transplant inspection, and maintenance of adequate ventilation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Insect-proof screens and properly sealed entrances are among the most important exclusion tools in nurseries and greenhouses, but their efficacy depends on installation quality, door management, transplant inspection, and maintenance of adequate ventilation.

items.text: Leaf removal should not be applied generally or randomly. It should be restricted to limited foci with active infestation while considering canopy cover, fruit protection, and natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Leaf removal should not be applied generally or randomly. It should be restricted to limited foci with active infestation while considering canopy cover, fruit protection, and natural enemies.

items.text: Collection of infested fruits is an important measure for preventing larvae from completing development and for reducing rots and market or processing losses.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Collection of infested fruits is an important measure for preventing larvae from completing development and for reducing rots and market or processing losses.

items.text: Postharvest crop-residue removal is also a major intervention and should be implemented through a system that includes fruit collection, crop termination, shredding, containment or treatment, equipment cleaning, and continued field monitoring.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Postharvest crop-residue removal is also a major intervention and should be implemented through a system that includes fruit collection, crop termination, shredding, containment or treatment, equipment cleaning, and continued field monitoring.

items.text: Shredding alone does not guarantee elimination of all pest stages; it is one stage within a sanitary-disposal system.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Shredding alone does not guarantee elimination of all pest stages; it is one stage within a sanitary-disposal system.

items.text: The relative importance of these methods varies with production system. Screens and isolation are essential in nurseries and greenhouses, whereas priorities in open fields are focus containment, collection of infested fruits, residue removal, and cleaning of equipment and containers.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The relative importance of these methods varies with production system. Screens and isolation are essential in nurseries and greenhouses, whereas priorities in open fields are focus containment, collection of infested fruits, residue removal, and cleaning of equipment and containers.
